item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I don't care for this, but ehh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Right, well, surprisingly this is still alive after 5 years of absence, as is my profile. I knew Hurley would make it, but this I thought would be gone.

If I find the time and motivation I might fix this (read: complete overhaul) in the next few weeks, just as thanks to those who kept it alive, then fade back into the ether for another 5 years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Symmetrical plague" sounds kind of silly. Also this would probably be the weirdest way for the world to end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has been one of my personal favorites for years, the mental image I get from it is spectacular.

The only jarring not for me is that the stone is written in an unknown language, and then a translated inscription is posted below it. The method of translation should be noted, sure we have SCiPs that could do it, but then state that. Or if a Rosetta stone was used, or if painstaking guesswork was done. Even modern Earth languages get garbled in translation to other languages, and this is an apparent E.T. script, perfectly transposed into English? The exploration of the method used to accomplish that is much more valuable than throwing D-class into SCP-1181.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First I thought it was neat…then it got so bogged down and simple that I hated it and was itching to downvote…..

Then I read the two tests, and even though I knew how it would go, the sheer SIZE of it made my jaw drop and made me like it.

"analysis of tests" should be cut. It adds nothing and is slightly contradictory.

The text on the thing saved it from downvote for me. I had been bothered by a "what the hell is this for" feeling…the idea that an entire civilization shared that feeling and sacrificed itself….the idea that the fire barrier (which had made no sense initially) WAS the SCP (or rather a second SCP surrounding the first) was great.

If this is ever cleaned up, I'll be glad to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still gonna upvote this, but someone really needs to change that the inscription of "unknown language" was still somehow decoded. Am I incorrect when I say that makes absolutely no sense at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this until the decoded inscription at the end, that ruined it for me. I liked it better as completely unexplained. Still upvoting, just thought I'd add my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my mind I could imagine thousands and thousand of people packed in a hemisphere and mingled with each other, creating bloody slurry like a certain scene from Duke Nukem 3D and I found it both amusing and creepy. This article definitely needs more upvote, and I'm adding one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really didn't understand this. What it is, what it does, how it works, who put it there, why… You don't need to answer all this, but as a whole it looks like you left it unfinished.

I'll upvote anyway because its nicely written and fairly interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heading to bed now, but noticed a few flaws ~still~ present throughout the article. Will have them fixed tomorrow.
Does get a +1 as it is, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but I don't think the last collapsible adds anything of value.

And if the language of the inscription is unknown, how was it decoded at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do you pass through the center of the circle if you have 3 other copies in PERFECT symmetry trying to do the same thing.
"It also cannot detected by any known instruments"

cannot BE detected?
Also there are instuments that can sense by touch :I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dankaar's comment is how I interpreted it.

Also,

but it's very confusing, and grossly mathematically inaccurate - I don't know how everyone's missed it.

Possibly because they didn't "miss" it? I know you've mentioned that you don't read replies to your comments, but frankly, if you're going to make statements like these while asking a question, at least stick around for the answer, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's three copies, plus the original, making four individuals in total, spaced 90 degrees from each other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do three copies spawn, or four? Throughout the article, it says that three copies spawn equidistant from each other, 90 degrees apart. However, if it was three copies that spawned, equidistant would be 120 degrees; likewise if equidistant was 90 degrees, it would be four copies spawning. Until the end, I thought the error was the 90 degrees and that three spawned, but now I'm not sure, so I won't change it myself, but it's very confusing, and grossly mathematically inaccurate - I don't know how everyone's missed it.

I'll do a few minor grammar edits, though. And for the whole, with the concept and such…it would be +1. But please fix it to make it mathematically accurate, whichever way it's supposed to be - with that in mind, novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm coming out of a really long hiatus just to up vote it. It's strangely appealing in a way I can't quite describe, but regardless I enjoyed the read. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's basically a kaleidoscope, but for real creatures? …Nice. It needs some work, but I'll give it a +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I agree that it needs a rewrite. It could be worse, but it could absolutely be better.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This article has been placed up for review, please come look. It needs more attention.

I still love the imagery presented in this one, but definitely agree that it needs more work. I think this could easily be saved with a rewrite, to give it a bit more punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're not allowed to have sigs here, just FYI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When a living animal, or portion of a living animal, passes through the center of SCP-1181, SCP-1181 and its barrier activates.

I think this segment clarifies that. Also, signatures are against the rules here, according to the required reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a prety good concept, but it needs some things. I read through it and not once did I see how it activates. That's what I want to know. How does it activate and what triggers the activation. You could at least say something like, "We do not currently know how it activates."

Just a tip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The combustion is tacked on. I think it would be far more interesting/squicky if the bodies were to simply press up against the sides, crushing everything to death. Also, what happens if more than one organism is inside the area when it activates?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better, the translation really adds a lot, although how the tablet got here is still kinda confusing. I'm not really a huge fan of the concept, but it's not bad, and it's well written, so enjoy your +.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have rewritten the article, changed a few things, thoughts? better/worse?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, and the execution's well done. I would suggest adding a diagram or a picture showing the area and the lines. That section is a little confusing if you ask me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm considering rewriting this article, and alot of those ideas are great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this.
While there's some nice imagery (the last bit with the object keeping symmetry was neat, as well as the mass of fused corpses - i'm a fan of body horror.) the article as a whole is rather boring.
The properties it has are unexplained and inexplicable, but in a way that doesn't quite spark any thoughts, or interest.

One thing that I'm thinking of that'd possibly make it better - indication that the semi-permeable membrane that combusts those attempting to leave are someone else's containment procedure, aimed at preventing thousands of clones of anything that enters flooding the outside world.
Also , why make it just work on people? Imagine a fly getting in, and replicating in the space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Wow. I've had my eyes skip over a few words, or a line, not a significant chunk of paragraph before. Question redacted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I just missed it, but is there a way to STOP this process once it begins? Otherwise you'd eventually have an Infinite D-Class mountain of body.

SCP-1181 will deactivate if the subject passes through the center point a second time, or dies. Should the subject die, they, all their copies, and any remains will disappear. Should the subject pass through the center for a second time, all copies, and any remains of the copies, will disappear, and any injuries the subject sustained will be healed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I envision this, I'm seeing a real life version of those flash games, where more and more things keep popping up that can't really harm you, you have to wipe them out, but the physics-boggling aspect of having so much STUFF in it at once starts to become equally amusing and horrifying. Gonna have to +1 to it, but I have a question.

Maybe I just missed it, but is there a way to STOP this process once it begins? Otherwise you'd eventually have an Infinite D-Class mountain of body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scp is EXTREMELY CREEPY. Loved the images it produced in my head. The mathematics twist and the futility to reach the center after many copies are made made it even better. The note is bad, though; it can do well without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, i've recently come to that conclusion, just something i was kicking around

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, you're right, a tacked on compulsion wouldn't help much. Still I might save it for a tale about some researcher who kept feeding D's into it to satisfy his entrancement or something.

As for the origin story, in my head its rather long, could i get away with making it a tale, or would it really need to be on the SCP?

And i've removed the last line of the doctors note, i would have removed the whole thing, but i realized i didn't have a note about symmetry anywhere else in the article, and it is important to the SCP. So i'll edit around and see if i can move it into the main description, or into the test logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i were to add another anomalous attribute to it that when it is watched from a birds eye view, the watcher becomes more and more addicted to it/entranced by it?

This is both ridiculous and tacked-on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although, and this is just an idea, if i were to add another anomalous attribute to it that when it is watched from a birds eye view, the watcher becomes more and more addicted to it/entranced by it?

FWIW, this is probably a bad idea. It doesn't really add anything, and borders on 'tacked-on compulsion' territory.

I have no idea what I think of that idea, but - also FWIW - I don't think adding an origin would be a bad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it survived the night, that's the best compliment i could get.

The general consensus seems to be that I remove the doctors note, so i think I'm going to do that, or edit it to make it less tacky.

Although, and this is just an idea, if i were to add another anomalous attribute to it that when it is watched from a birds eye view, the watcher becomes more and more addicted to it/entranced by it? It seems like overkill on and already reasonably long article, but it could add something i think. Maybe it could be a tale, then again, if it is an attribute of the SCP it needs to be on the main article… Thoughts??

I have another idea involving its origin story, but I'm unsure whether i should do that as a tale or add that in as well. The basic concept of its origin would be something like a crossover between an alien recreation, where aliens watched it from space or something, crossed with it being an ancient warriors coming of age right (surviving it)

Thoughts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That note is terrible. The underlying concept of the article is decent enough, but the execution is lacking, especially in spots like descriptions of the sensations and so forth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… I still just don't have an opinion on this. The writing is not bad, and I don't think it merits being kicked off the wiki, but I don't find it interesting, myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is kind of ridiculous, conceptually. It's an interesting thought exercise, but other than that I don't much care for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disliked the doctor's note at the end. I could probably have done without the bursting into flame upon contact with the inside surface of the sphere, too. On the other hand, this has high WTF content, and there's a certain charm to the idea of a spontaneous, improvised Busby Berkeley number in which some of the participants occasionally fuse into horrible, suffering flesh blobs. So: no vote at the moment; still thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely written. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually that's a good idea, I'll change it now

Thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the note would work if it was not brought up like an official research note, but more as a personal log entry or something. It'd make me feel better.

Still going to give this a vote. It's not bad, and the concept is neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not entirely sure how I feel about this, yet. It's an interesting concept, though. The doctor's note does seem somewhat unprofessional, though the note on radial symmetry is an interesting detail.

Reserving my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks guys.

I fixed those bits up soul.

As for the doctors note… I don't know if i can remove it, for many reasons, mostly personal, I just think that small bit of dark contrast sums up the SCP, for me anyway. I'll wait and see what people think before i do anything, if people really decide it needs to be changed or removed, then I'll do it.

Anyway, thanks again.

EDIT: And thanks to everyone who had a part in helping me edit and get this ready, i really appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Swampless! Good to see your SCP up.

Living fused and/or mutilated copies and copies that block each others' paths, will still attempt to the best of their ability to mimic

You don't need the comma here.

Should the subject die, they and all their copies, and any remains, of the copies, will disappear.

You don't need some commas here. 'Should the subject die, they, all their copies, and any remains will disappear'.

there were over a 16 thousand copies

Remove 'a'

I don't really think you need the doctor's note, but I like this one. I also think it'd be a great place to do some synchronized dancing in. Just imagine Thriller in here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I'd get rid of the last sentence in the doctor's note, but I like how this turned out.

Edit: And I got a kick out of the title on the series list.
